  • ✅ Initially works
  • ⚠️ Stops working after some time (session inconsistency)

3. Desktop (Laptops)

  • ✅ Works reliably in Chrome
  • ⚠️ In Microsoft Edge, tiles sometimes fail to load initially but work after refresh

Key Issues Identified

1. Inconsistent Behavior

  • Same application behaves differently:
    • Across devices (iOS vs Android)
    • Across browsers (Edge vs Chrome)
    • Across sessions (intermittent failures)

2. Blank Screen Issue

  • Observed in both:
    • Browser (after successful launch)
    • Joule Work app (application shell loads, but content is empty)

3. Strong Browser Dependency

  • Chrome on iOS consistently fails
  • Edge and Google app perform better

4. Mobile App Instability (Joule Work)

  • iOS: Not working
  • Android: Works intermittently

Architectural Observations

Based on the current setup, the issue appears to be architectural rather than purely configuration-related.

1. Multi-Layer Authentication Complexity

  • Authentication via Azure AD
  • Trust and session handling via IAS
  • Backend access via Principal Propagation (Web Dispatcher)

This creates:

  • Multiple token exchanges
  • Session dependencies across layers
  • Increased chances of session misalignment

2. Session Handling in Mobile Environments

  • SAP Build Work Zone relies on:
    • Browser-based sessions
    • Cookies and storage

Mobile platforms (especially iOS):

  • Restrict cookies (SameSite policies)
  • Isolate sessions in WebView containers

Result:

  • Session loss
  • Token expiration not handled gracefully
  • Blank screen scenarios

3. Joule Work App Limitations

  • Likely uses a WebView container
  • WebView environments:
    • Do not fully share authentication context with browser sessions

Particularly problematic in:

  • Multi-hop authentication (Azure AD → IAS → Work Zone)

4. Distributed Runtime Dependencies

Application flow includes:

  • Work Zone
  • HTML5 App Repository
  • Destination Service
  • Web Dispatcher
  • Backend system

👉 Any intermittent failure in this chain:

  • Results in incomplete UI rendering
  • No clear error feedback to user

5. iOS Platform Constraints

  • All browsers use WebKit
  • Strict enforcement of:
    • Cookie handling
    • Session persistence

 Leading to:

  • Chrome not working
  • Inconsistent app behavior

Conclusion

While the application performs well under stable conditions, the current setup shows:

  • ❌ Inconsistent behavior across devices and browsers
  • ❌ Limited reliability for mobile usage
  • ⚠️ High dependency on session handling across multiple layers

This makes the solution not fully stable for productive mobile usage at this stage.

Questions to the SAP Community

I would appreciate insights on the following:

  1. Is this behavior a known limitation when using:

    • Azure AD + IAS
    • Work Zone + Joule Work app
    • Principal Propagation via Web Dispatcher

  2. Is Joule Work app officially recommended for:

    • Work Zone HTML5/Fiori apps in such architectures?

  3. What is the recommended mobile strategy?

    • SAP Mobile Start?
    • SAP MDK-based applications?

  1. Are there best practices for:

    • Stabilizing session handling across Azure AD and IAS?
    • Avoiding blank screen issues in Work Zone apps?

  2. Has anyone implemented a similar architecture successfully with consistent mobile behavior?

Final Thought

This experience highlights an important architectural insight:

SAP Build Work Zone works reliably in browser-based environments, but mobile usage—especially with external authentication providers and multi-hop architectures—requires careful alignment with SAP’s recommended mobile strategy.

entry.title: What’s new in Mobile development kit client 26.6
entry.content:
Dear Community,

I'm happy to announce that a new release of the Mobile development kit (MDK) is now available for all SAP Build and Mobile Services customers. You can download it from the SAP Software Center, or soon from the community download page.

The SAP Mobile Services Client has been updated to version 26.6.0 on the Google Play Store and will soon be updated on the Apple App Store.

SAP MDK enables developers and technical business users to build cross-platform mobile applications. It allows you to build your application once, in an integrated development environment (SAP Business Application Studio / Visual Studio Code) and run it natively on Mobile (iOS & Android).  

The following UI enhancements are available only on iOS and Android platforms:

  • Security & Compliance Enhancements
    • Pre-Onboarding Device Compliance Check
    • App Attestation Support
  • Embedded AI Enhancements
    • AI Notice Form Cell Control
    • AI Feedback Form Cell Control
  • UI Enhancements
    • Stepper Form Cell Control
    • Enhanced Offline OData Progress Feedback
    • Configurable Onboarding Illustration Sizes for Android
    • New Section Filter Bar with Advanced Filtering
    • Action Bar Image Icon Size Customization
  • Client API Enhancements
    • Dynamic Visibility and Enablement for Button Table Buttons

Pre-Onboarding Device Compliance Check

MDK already provides a device compliance check API which can be used in the application metadata after a user completes onboarding. With this release, MDK extends this capability to perform compliance checks before onboarding begins.

Key Benefits:

  • Early detection of potentially compromised devices (for example, rooted or jailbroken devices).
  • Reduced risk of exposing user credentials and corporate data to non-compliant devices.
  • Stronger security posture through compliance validation at the earliest stage of user access.
  • Flexible administrator controls to determine how non-compliant devices are handled.
  • Support for organizational security policies through configurable actions such as Block or Warn.
  • Built-in test mode for validating compliance-check behavior without requiring an actual rooted or jailbroken device.

// Excerpt of BrandedSettings.json
"PreLoginDeviceComplianceCheck": {
    "Enabled": true,
    "FailureAction": "Warn"
}​
  • The FailureAction setting determines what happens when a compromised device is detected:
    • Block – The user is shown a message indicating that the device does not meet security requirements and cannot continue with onboarding.
    • Warn – The user is shown a warning message but can choose to continue with onboarding after acknowledging the alert.

A Customized message in case of blocking users access the app.

 

A Customized message in case of warning users and continuing using the app.


For more information, refer to this documentation.

App Attestation Support

MDK now supports App Attestation, allowing SAP Mobile Services to validate that requests originate from an approved app running on a trusted device. This capability provides an additional layer of protection against app impersonation and unauthorized modifications.

Key Benefits:

  • Reduces the risk of requests originating from altered or cloned app installations.
  • Provides a reliable way for mobile services to establish trust in the source of incoming requests.
  • Supports enterprise security and compliance initiatives.
  • Works with existing MDK applications with minimal configuration changes.

App Attestation is configured through the Attestation property in the MDKProject.json file. The create-client tool automatically applies the required platform-specific settings during client generation.

//Excerpt of MDKProject.json
{
  "AppName": "YourAppName",
  "BundleID": "com.example.yourapp",
  "AppVersion": "1.0.0",
  "Attestation": {
    "iOS": {
      "Environment": "development",
      "DebugToken": "your-ios-debug-token-here"
    },
    "Android": {
      "DebugToken": "your-android-debug-token-here"
    }
  }
}

Notes:

  • Debug tokens are intended for development and testing only and are not recommended for production deployments.
  • Before configuring attestation in your MDK project, an administrator must enable and configure the Attestation feature in the mobile services admin UI. See configuration settings for iOS and Android.

    For more information, refer to this documentation.

AI Notice Form Cell Control

As part of ongoing enhancements in embedded AI capabilities, a new FormCell control, AINoticeFormCell, is introduced to display an inline notice for AI-generated content that may require review before use. 

Key Benefits:

  • Clearly indicates when content is generated using AI.
  • Helps users understand that AI-generated content may need validation.
  • Supports optional links for additional context or guidance.

The notice shows an AI icon, a customizable message, and an optional hyperlink. The hyperlink can be configured to open supporting information or trigger an MDK action when selected. A common use case is providing details about the AI feature, including usage guidelines, privacy information, or related policies.

//Excerpt of a .page
{
  "_Name": "ainotice_with_hyperlink",
  "_Type": "Control.Type.FormCell.AINotice",
  "IsVisible": true,
  "Separator": true,
  "Message": "This is an AI generated message, proceed with caution.",
  "HyperlinkText": "Learn more",
  "OnHyperlinkPress": "/MDK266/Actions/Message.action",
  "Styles": {
    "Background": "AINoticeYellowBackground",
    "Message": "AINoticeHeadlineBold"
  }
},

The AINoticeFormCellProxy class enables dynamic updates at runtime, including changes to the message, hyperlink text, styling, and click behavior through MDK rules.

let control = context.evaluateTargetPathForAPI('#Page:AINoticeClientAPI/#Control:ainotice_api_target');
  let message = control.getMessage();
   control.setMessage('Updated via Client API!');
   let hyperlinkText = control.getHyperlinkText();
     control.setHyperlinkText('Updated hyperlink');
       control.setStyles({
    "Background": "AINoticeYellowBackground",
    "Message": "AINoticeBlueMessage"
  });

AI Notice FormCell control examples
For more information, refer to this documentation.

AI Feedback Form Cell Control

As part of the embedded AI enhancement area, a new FormCell control, AIFeedback, is introduced to enable quick user feedback on AI-generated content. The control provides thumbs-up and thumbs-down options along with a caption and is designed specifically for AI-related experiences.

Key Benefits:

  • Enables quick user feedback on AI-generated content
  • Captures user sentiment in a simple, lightweight way
  • Helps improve AI experiences through user input
//Excerpt of a .page
{
  "_Type": "Control.Type.FormCell.AIFeedback",
  "_Name": "SectionFunc_InitUp",
  "Caption": "Initial value: Up",
  "Value": "Up",
  "IsEditable": true,
  "IsVisible": true,
  "Separator": true,
  "Styles": {
    "Background": "AIFeedbackBackground",
    "Caption": "AIFeedbackCaption",
    "VoteButtons": "AIFeedbackVoteButton"
  }
},

Note: This control does not provide any AI capabilities. It is a UI component used to capture feedback in AI-enabled scenarios, and developers determine what to do with the feedback and where to store it for their application.

The AIFeedbackFormCellProxy class enables dynamic updates at runtime, including changes to the caption, vote value, and styles through MDK rules.

AI Feedback Form cell control examples

For more information, refer to this documentation.

Stepper Form Cell Control

A new FormCell control, Stepper, is introduced to allow users to increase or decrease numeric values using buttons or direct input. The control supports configurable step size, value range limits, decimal precision, and validation.

Key Benefits:

  • Enables easy adjustment of numeric values through stepper buttons or manual entry
  • Supports configurable increments for flexible use cases
  • Allows enforcement of minimum and maximum value limits
  • Provides control over numeric precision for consistent input formatting
{
  "Caption": "Initial Value 5 and Step 1",
  "Value": 5,
  "DecimalPlacesSupported": 2,
  "Step": 1,
  "_Type": "Control.Type.FormCell.Stepper",
  "_Name": "StepperWithValue"
}

The StepperFormCellProxy class enables dynamic updates at runtime, including retrieves or modifies the Stepper value through MDK rules. 

Fiori Stepper Form cell control with its OnValueChange event

For more information, refer to this documentation.

Enhanced Offline OData Progress Feedback

Offline OData operations now provide step-level progress messages, offering improved visibility into long-running tasks such as initialization, download, upload, and send store. Localized default messages are displayed automatically with no additional configuration.

Key Benefits:

  • Introduces step-level progress updates for offline operations
  • Improves visibility into long-running processes such as sync and data transfer
  • Provides localized default messages out of the box
  • Enables customization and runtime control of progress feedback
  • Progress messages can be customized using the ProgressMessages property under OfflineOptions in the .service definition. These messages are used during offline operations including initialization, download, upload, and send store.
//.service file
{
  "DestinationName": "com.sap.edm.sampleservice.v4",
  "OfflineEnabled": true,
  "OfflineOptions": {
    "AutoIncludeProgressMessages": false,
    "ProgressMessages": {
      "BuildingEntityStore": "{0}/{1} Custom Building the entity store",
      "CreatingBootStrapDatabase": "{0}/{1} Custom Creating bootstrap database",
      "CreatingRequestQueueDatabase": "{0}/{1} Custom Creating request queue database",
      "DownloadingEntityStore": "{0}/{1} Custom Downloading entity store {2}",
      "ErasingExpiredRequests": "{0}/{1} Custom Erasing expired requests",
      "LoadingMetadata": "{0}/{1} Custom Performing request queue optimizer",
      "PerformingTransactionMerge": "{0}/{1} Custom Performing transaction builder",
      "PerformingCreateDeleteMerge": "{0}/{1} Custom Performing undo local creation optimizer",
      "ProcessingDefiningQueries": "{0}/{1} Custom Processing defining queries {2}",
      "ProcessingRequests": "{0}/{1} Custom Processing requests {2}",
      "ReapplyingChanges": "{0}/{1} Custom Reapplying changes",
      "AnalyzingReceivedData": "{0}/{1} Custom Receiving data from the client",
      "ReceivingDataFromServer": "{0}/{1} Custom Receiving data from the server {2}",
      "RemovingDeletedRelationships": "{0}/{1} Custom Removing deleted relationships",
      "RemovingRemoveAfterUploadRequests": "{0}/{1} Custom Removing RemoveAfterUpload requests",
      "SendingDataToServer": "{0}/{1} Custom Sending data to the server {2}",
      "SendingEntityStoreDatabase": "{0}/{1} Custom Sending entity store database {2}/{3}",
      "SendingRequestQueueDatabase": "{0}/{1} Custom Sending request queue database",
      "UploadPreprocessing": "{0}/{1} Custom Pre-processing requests before uploading",
      "WaitingForDownload": "{0}/{1} Custom Waiting for download"
    }
  },
  "OnOfflineProgressUpdate": "/MDK266/Rules/ProgressFeedback/OnOfflineProgressPromiseUpdate.js"
}

Notes: 

  • To control automatic UI behavior, use the AutoIncludeProgressMessages property.
  • A new OnOfflineProgressUpdate rule callback allows applications to  customize the progress messages at runtime. Structured event details can be accessed using the getEventData() client API.
  • ProgressText in the Initialize action is deprecated as of MDK 26.6.0. Use the new ProgressMessages instead. If both properties are configured, ProgressMessages takes precedence.

Granular progress feedback for Offline OData Initialization action

For more information, refer to this documentation.

Configurable Onboarding Illustration Sizes for Android

This enhancement is particularly useful on Android devices with limited screen space. For example, on smaller devices, onboarding illustrations can reduce the visible area available for important content such as passcode instructions and client policies. Adjusting the illustration size—or hiding it entirely—helps ensure critical information remains accessible and easier to read.

A new onboardingIllustrationSize property is available under OnboardingCustomizations in BrandedSettings.json, allowing you to control the size of illustrations displayed on onboarding screens or hide them entirely.

//Excerpt of BrandedSettings.json
"OnboardingCustomizations": {
  "onboardingIllustrationSize": "L", // XS, S, M, L, XL, None
},

This property is supported on Android for the following onboarding screens:

  • Launch
  • Set Passcode
  • Confirm Passcode
  • Notification Permission
  • Camera Permission
  • Offline Network Error

Key Benefits

  • Provides greater flexibility when designing onboarding experiences.
  • Helps optimize screen space on smaller Android devices.
  • Improves visibility of onboarding content and client policies.
  • Allows illustrations to be resized or removed based on branding and usability requirements.

    Examples showcasing onboarding illustration on Create passcode screen


    For more information, refer to this documentation.

New Section Filter Bar with Advanced Filtering

MDK now provides a new FilterBar experience for sectioned tables, enabling more advanced filtering scenarios while following SAP Fiori design patterns.

While the existing Filter Feedback Bar remains ideal for quick-access filters and common filtering actions, the new FilterBar supports richer interactions for scenarios that require more sophisticated input. Users can work with controls such as segmented selections, list pickers, switches, sliders, and multi-sort options directly within the filtering experience.

Key Benefits

  • Supports more advanced filtering scenarios beyond simple filter chips.
  • Provides a consistent experience aligned with SAP Fiori design guidelines.
  • Reduces the need for custom filtering screens and dialogs.
  • Enables users to refine large datasets more efficiently.
  • Supports a variety of filter interaction patterns, including selection lists, ranges, toggles, and sorting options.

Filter bar at one or more sections

 

//Excerpt of a .page
{
  "Sections": [
    {
      "FilterBar": {
        "_Type": "Control.Type.FilterBar",
        "_Name": "SortFilter",
        "Filters": "/MDK266/Rules/FilterBar/InitialFilters.js",
        "Sections": [
          {
            "_Name": "FilterSection1",
            "Controls": [
              {
                "_Name": "Width",
                "_Type": "FilterBar.Type.Segmented",
                "Label": "DimensionWidth Compare",
                "Items": [
                  {
                    "DisplayValue": "< 25",
                    "ReturnValue": "DimensionWidth lt 25"
                  }
                ],
                "AllowEmptySelection": true,
                "AllowMultipleSelection": false,
                "DisplayOnBar": "/MDK266/Rules/FilterBar/GetDisplayOnBarValue.js"
              },
              {
                "_Name": "PriceLP",
                "_Type": "FilterBar.Type.ListPicker",
                "Label": "Price >=",
                "Value": "Price ge 126",
                "PickerItems": {
                  "DisplayValue": "{Price}",
                  "ReturnValue": "/MDK266/Rules/FilterBar/PriceReturnValue.js",
                  "Target": {
                    "EntitySet": "Products",
                    "Service": "/MDK266/Services/SampleServiceV4Online.service",
                    "QueryOptions": "$top=30"
                  }
                },
                "DisplayOnBar": "/MDK266/Rules/FilterBar/GetDisplayOnBarValue.js"
              },
              {
                "_Name": "Favorite",
                "_Type": "FilterBar.Type.Switch",
                "Label": "Is Favorite?",
                "HelperText": "Favorite is",
                "_SimpleQuery": {
                  "Property": "Category"
                },
                "DisplayOnBar": "/MDK266/Rules/FilterBar/GetDisplayOnBarValue.js"
              },
              {
                "_Name": "Height",
                "_Type": "FilterBar.Type.Slider",
                "Label": "Height >=",
                "MinValue": 10,
                "MaxValue": 111,
                "Step": 1,
                "SimpleQuery": {
                  "Property": "DimensionHeight",
                  "Operator": "ge"
                },
                "DisplayOnBar": "/MDK266/Rules/FilterBar/GetDisplayOnBarValue.js"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      },
      "_Name": "Section",
      "_Type": "Section.Type.ObjectTable"
    }
  ],

Notes: 

  • This new FilterBar is defined at the section level which is more intuitive.

  • If there is only one section, the FilterBar will remain sticky (i.e., it will stay at the top of the screen while scrolling). If there are multiple sections, it will scroll with the content. Users can also define different FilterBars for individual sections, unlike the existing FilterFeedbackBar. 
    Additionally, both approaches cannot coexist on the same page; FilterBar will take higher priority.
     
    For more information, refer to this documentation.

Action Bar Image Icon Size Customization

You can now control the display size of image icons in the Action Bar using the new Width property on ActionBarItem. By specifying a width value, image icons can be displayed at a custom size instead of the default 18 × 18 dimensions.

Key Benefits:

  • Provides greater control over Action Bar appearance.
  • Supports larger image icons for improved visibility.
  • Enables more flexibility when implementing custom branding and designs.

    Action Bar item Width property examples

     

"ActionBar": {
  "Caption": "Icon Width Examples",
  "Items": [
    {
      "_Name": "IconDefault",
      "Position": "Right",
      "Icon": "/MDK266/Images/actionbar.png",
      "OnPress": "/MDK266/Actions/Message.action"
    },
    {
      "_Name": "Icon24",
      "Position": "Right",
      "Icon": "/MDK266/Images/actionbar.png",
      "Width": 24,
      "OnPress": "/MDK266/Actions/Message.action"
    },
    {
      "_Name": "Icon36",
      "Position": "Right",
      "Icon": "/MDK266/Images/actionbar.png",
      "Width": 36
    }
  ]
},​

Notes:

  • The maximum supported value is 36; values above this limit are automatically set to 36.
  • Values less than or equal to 0 use the default size of 18.
  • This property applies only to image icons and does not affect SAP icons or font icons.
  • When sizing your image, pay attention to any existing white space bordering the image.

For more information, refer to this documentation.

Dynamic Visibility and Enablement for Button Table Buttons

The ButtonTableButtonProxy now supports new client APIs that allow applications to read and dynamically control the enabled state and visibility of Button Table buttons at runtime.

Key Benefits:

  • Enables dynamic control of button availability and visibility.
  • Supports context-aware user experiences.
  • Allows button states to be updated through rules based on application logic.
  • Provides APIs to both read and modify button state at runtime.

Section Button visibility and Enablement

 

Available APIs:

  • setEnabled() — Enables or disables the button.
  • setVisible() — Shows or hides the button.
  • getEnabled() — Returns the current enabled state.
  • getVisible() — Returns the current visibility state.
export default function ToggleButtonEnabled(context) {
  let pageProxy = context.getPageProxy();
  let button = pageProxy.getControl('SectionedTable').getSection('TargetSection').getButton('ButtonTableTypeButton0');
  if (button) {
    let currentEnabled = button.getEnabled();
    button.setEnabled(!currentEnabled);
    let currentVisible = button.getVisible();
    button.setVisible(!currentVisible);
  }

Become an SAP MDK Explorer!
Do you have a requirement to build a mobile solution for your enterprise? Take your first steps today by following these tutorials to get started and learn more about the SAP Mobile development kit. We’re excited to see what you build—your feedback and ideas are invaluable!

Submit Improvement Requests
You can submit your improvement request here for Mobile development kit to the SAP Customer Influence site, a central place for all product improvement requests. You can browse all improvement requests, submit improvement requests, comment, vote, receive updates, and see who has voted at the SAP Customer Influence site.

entry.title: Your Voice on the Future of SAP UI Technology
entry.content:
AI is reshaping how enterprise applications are built, used, and experienced. This raises fundamental questions for SAP's UI technology strategy - and we want to hear from you.

TL;DR: We are running a short, anonymous survey on the future of enterprise UI development with SAP. It takes 5 minutes and is open from July 1 to July 19, 2026. Take the survey →

What are we asking?

We have put together a short survey on the future of enterprise UI development with SAP. It covers topics such as:

  • How well SAPUI5 meets your needs today - and in the age of AI
  • Which UI experiences you expect to dominate enterprise apps in the coming years
  • Which capabilities matter most for your future development work
  • Where you see SAP's UI technology strategy heading

Why your input matters

SAP's UI frameworks power thousands of enterprise applications worldwide. Decisions about their future direction affect you — whether you are building new apps, maintaining existing ones, or advising customers on their architecture. Your perspective as a developer, architect, or decision maker in the SAP ecosystem is what we need to make the right calls.

Who should take this survey?

This survey is for anyone working with SAP UI technologies: SAPUI5 / Fiori developers, UI and UX architects, technical leads, enterprise architects, and IT decision makers at SAP customers and partners.

Call to Action

Make your voice heard by participating in the survey here: SAP UI Technology Strategy Survey. It only takes 5 minutes to complete, and is open from July 1 to July 19, 2026.

entry.title: Using RFC Function Modules and BAPIs in SAP Build Process Automation
entry.content:
Hi Everyone!

Recently, I was working on a requirement to generate an SD Billing Document in SAP S/4HANA using SAP Build Process Automation.

Due to some internal requirements & technical restrictions, the only option available to me was to use the BAPI_BILLINGDOC_CREATEMULTIPLE BAPI to create the billing document.

To use this BAPI in SAP Build, there are two main configurations required:

1. Map the virtual host to the internal host in SAP Cloud Connector using the RFC protocol, and expose the required BAPI or RFC function module.

2. Create a destination of type RFC in the SAP BTP Cockpit.

Let's first see how Cloud to On-Premises mapping in Cloud Connector looks like:

We also need to enable the required resources, such as the BAPIs and function modules that we want to consume in SAP Build. The RFC_FUNCTION_SEARCH function module enables the search for function modules from SAP Build.



And this is how the destination in BTP looks:

The virtual message server is configured in the Cloud Connector and mapped to the internal SAP S/4HANA host, and logon group information is available in SMLG.



Logon group can be found here :

Now that the Cloud Connector and BTP destination configurations are complete, let’s log in to SAP Build and see how we can create an action using BAPI_BILLINGDOC_CREATEMULTIPLE.

Select RFC's, BAPI's:



All the BAPIs & RFCs from backend will be available to use. We will go ahead and look for BAPI/RFC we'd like to use, and create a new project.

The action is now available in Connectors -> Actions, and can be used in our build projects.



Using this action, I created a custom process that can be triggered to generate a billing document by entering the reference sales document number.



Let's see it in action;
Assume we have below sales order, for which we'd like to generate a billing document.



Let’s now access the custom process we created. I have added the form URL to SAP Build Work Zone for easy access.




Since I added an email notification step to the process, we receive an email once the billing document is generated successfully.



Let's validated the generated billing document number in the system.



That concludes the demo. Thanks for reading!

Stay tuned for more demos on AI agents using Joule Studio, n8n, and automation in SAP Build.

entry.title: Adding a Custom Column in Fiori Elements (List Report + Object Page)
entry.content:
Introduction 

The purpose of this blog post is to specify the elements to add in order to set up this custom column, and more specifically to understand the path to configure in the manifest when you want to modify a table, because using this extension in an association facet of an Object Page can be more complicated to understand.
To do so, two elements must be implemented: first, the column definition through an XML fragment, and second, its registration in the manifest file.

I had to use this extension because I was asked to customize the data display by applying CSS styles, such as changing the color depending on the displayed value.
This blog post also aims to specify when to use this extension and why it is preferable not to use it, along with an example of a regression I experienced during its addition.

This column can be added either to a List Report page or to an Object Page. Each table type has its own extension name, and the implementation differs slightly depending on the table type.

       Why use this extension as a last resort?

In this blog post, we will look at how to add a custom column. However, other approaches can often meet the requirement. This extension should be considered only when no standard alternative is available, for the following reasons:

  • Fiori Elements Flexibility: Some requirements can be fulfilled through manifest customization or OData annotations without writing any JavaScript or XML code.
  • Maintainability: View extensions are custom code that must be maintained whenever the Fiori Elements framework is updated, whereas standard annotations are automatically handled by SAP.
  • Regression Risk: A poorly implemented extension can interfere with native table features such as sorting, filtering, Excel export, and P13n personalization.

Example of a regression I encountered with this extension:

While developing my XML, I needed to add a specific width to my column. However, by defining this width in pixels (px), I caused a regression in the native behavior, which uses rem units. For example, the display variants were altered depending on the screen size, because rem units rely on a relative value, unlike pixels which impose a fixed size.

      Table Types and Associated Extensions

Fiori Elements supports several table types. Each has its own extension name that must be declared in the manifest.

Table Type 

Extension Name 

Description

ResponsiveTable

ResponsiveTableColumnsExtension

ResponsiveTableCellsExtension

Responsive table adapted for mobile and desktop (default)

GridTable

GridTableColumnsExtension

Table with fixed columns, suitable for large datasets

TreeTable

TreeTableColumnsExtension

Hierarchical (tree-like) table

Important: For a ResponsiveTable, two extensions are required: one for the column header and one for the cell content.

How to Implement the Column?

     XML Extension

To add this column, you need to define a fragment for the view extension. Create a file in the webapp/ext/fragments/ folder to add any desired column.

In this fragment, you define the desired properties of the column (position, displayed element, etc.). You can also add P13N parameters, such as sorting, to enable users to sort the column.

Example XML extension:

 

      Manifest Extension

In the manifest.json file, declare the view extension and the application parameters. Create an extension corresponding to the table type on the desired entity, then specify the fragment name and type.

Example Manifest:

Result :

After adding the XML extension and declaring it in the manifest.json, a new column appears in the table. In the example, the column customizes data display by applying CSS styles, such as changing colors based on displayed values.

The document also highlights a date-coloring scenario where different colors indicate whether a deadline is distant, approaching, or overdue. In such cases, a view extension is generally unnecessary because OData Criticality annotations can provide the same functionality in a standard, Fiori Elements–compliant way. 

Therefore, this standard approach should be preferred whenever they satisfy the requirement.

Adding a Column to an Object Page

      Differences Compared to a List Report

An Object Page can contain multiple sections, each with its own table. The main differences are:

Aspect

List Report

Object Page

Target View 

ListReport.view.ListReport

ObjectPage.view.Details

Extension key

TypeTable + '|' + EntitySet

TypeTable + '|' + EntitySet + FacetID

Number of Tables 

One main table

Multiple tables (one per section)

Configuration Scope 

Entity-wide

Specific to each section/facet

      Example in an Object Page

In this example, a custom column is added to a Responsive Table within the Object Page of the Category entity. 

The custom column contains an Order button that is displayed only when the product stock is below 30 units. This behavior relies on business logic that cannot be implemented using standard Fiori Elements annotations. 

As with the Grid Table example, an extension fragment is created for the column definition:

However, because this is a Responsive Table, a second extension is required to define the column header:

Since the extension is used within an Object Page, the manifest.json configuration differs slightly from a List Report. You must target the ObjectPage.view.Details view and specify the corresponding facet in the extension key to indicate which table should receive the custom column.

The point I spent time on due to a lack of understanding: 'TypeTable' corresponds to the CDS of our table and not the CDS of the Object Page. In our example, 'Products' is a CDS association (added in a 'ProductsFacet' facet) of our application, which is based on the 'Category' CDS.


Result: 

 

Purpose of the Extension

     When Should You Use This Extension?

This extension is relevant in the following scenarios:

  • Complex conditional display: color, icon, or text based on multi-field business logic that cannot be expressed through annotations.
  • Non-standard UI5 component: action button, link, image, slider, or any component not natively supported by UI.DataField.
  • External data integration: displaying data coming from another JSON model or a separate REST API call. 
  • Frontend-side calculation: combining or calculating values using a JavaScript formatter. 

     Best Practices 

  • Always prefix IDs: avoid conflicts with IDs generated by Fiori Elements. 
  • Use i18n: never hardcode header texts; always use entries from the i18n.properties file.
  • Keep JavaScript out of fragments: complex logic should be implemented in a dedicated formatter.js file, not inline in the XML.
  • Test P13n (personalization): ensure that the custom column behaves correctly with sorting, filtering, and show/hide functionalities.

entry.title: The GTT Route Simulator in SAP Build
entry.content:
About a year ago I published a blog post describing an integrated container scenario built with several SAP solutions—SAP Transportation Management, BN4L Freight Collaboration, and BN4L Global Track & Trace (GTT)—all running in the Industry Template Plus environment. To make that scenario feel a bit more realistic, I created a small SAP Build application that simulates an ocean-liner’s tracking information: it sends events to GTT so that an overseas voyage can be “played back” in just five minutes.

Using this tool—aptly named the GTT Route Simulator—the journey from the Port of Guangzhou to the Port of Rotterdam consists of:

  1. Proof-of-pickup, loading-start, loading-end and departure events are reported in Guangzhou.
  2. Location-update events are sent every few seconds while the vessel is at sea.
  3. Upon arrival in Rotterdam, arrival, unloading-start, unloading-end and proof-of-delivery events are reported.

When the simulator finishes, the shipment tracked event log in Global Track & Trace resembles a real shipment history, only compressed in time:

Building the Route Simulator with SAP Build

I put the first version together in a very short time—mainly to explore SAP Build’s capabilities. For anyone new to it, SAP Build is SAP’s low-code portfolio on SAP Business Technology Platform that lets business users and developers:

  • Create apps (SAP Build Apps)
    • Automate processes (SAP Build Process Automation)
    • Build digital workspaces (SAP Build Work Zone)

All of this is done with drag-and-drop tooling and enterprise-grade governance. The main program flow looks as follows:

The GTT Route Simulator is launched via a simple form where the user selects a scenario and a departure date/time. At the moment there are three configured scenarios:

  • Guangzhou → Rotterdam (standard ocean route)
    • Guangzhou → Rotterdam with a delay in the Suez Canal
    • Rotterdam → Saarbrücken (on-carriage - road leg)

Based on the selection, the main flow chooses a path and calls a subprocess that reports the loading events. Opening the sub process for the departure and loading events, demonstrates the steps that are executed:

  • A small JavaScript snippet prepares the data that must be embedded in the XML message sent to GTT.
  • A predefined action then transmits the XML payload to the GTT tenant.
  • The subprocess proceeds to the next event, and when finished, returns to the main flow.

The next subprocess handles periodic GPS updates to create the illusion of the vessel moving across the ocean. Once Rotterdam is reached, arrival and unloading events are posted.

Exception Handling: The Delay Scenario

Transport is a business of exceptions, so the second scenario is identical to the first until the vessel reaches the Suez Canal. There, the simulator injects a 5-day delay event that leads to the delayed status of the Freight Booking. This same delay scenario is currently used quite often for demonstrating the Transportation Reschedule Agent—a powerful AI-driven rescheduling feature, also developed within the IT Plus scope.

Final-Mile Scenario

The third scenario covers on-carriage from Rotterdam to a warehouse in Saarbrücken. I intentionally built in a three-hour delay because, in many demos, colleagues (or me) manually trigger the final transport and warehouse events. If some of those events are skipped, the simulator ensures the road freight order still completes so we don’t leave “open” documents cluttering the demo system—a little clean-desk policy for our landscape.

Why a Simulator?

The GTT Route Simulator does exactly what its name suggests—it mimics the tracking information normally provided by an LSP or directly by an ocean carrier. While SAP Build could ultimately retrieve real events and forward them to GTT, in production you would usually choose an integration platform such as SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) for that task. The simulator is therefore a lightweight, rapid-prototyping tool that accelerates demos and proofs of concept.

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Build: Q1 & Q2 2026 Release Highlights
entry.content:
Greetings, Builders! We blinked and just like that, the first half of 2026 is complete. There’s been a whirlwind of updates, including our recent announcement at SAPPHIRE of the new Joule Studio for intent-based development and agent creation.

Q1 and Q2 also brought a wave of innovations across SAP Build, helping our users extend and automate applications faster than ever. From AI-powered capabilities in SAP Build Work Zone, Joule Work Mobile, CAP, and SAPUI5, to improved visibility in process automation and more, these enhancements address some of your key pain points around developer productivity, simplified workflows, and user experience. Here are the Q1 and Q2 2026 release highlights from SAP Build, plus a look ahead at the new Joule Studio and how you can start learning the fundamentals of AI Agent building today.

Improved Customization and Gen AI in SAP Build Work Zone

No, it's not a social networking site from 2003, it's personalized, relevant SAP content.

Easy access to personalized content with My Space

Following the clear feedback from customers, it’s now possible to organize and manage your favorite Work Zone content on a dedicated, personal page. Many business users often have multiple roles, which may include content spanned across various spaces and structures. The “My Space” capability lets you bring all of that together on one page, allowing you to quickly access your relevant content and easily edit it to fit your specific needs.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @ThomasHensel

Enhanced text generation and refinement with AI

The text widget toolbar now includes an AI option to generate text for the widget. In addition, AI can refine the text in input fields, text areas, and text widgets. This includes changing the tone of the text, adjusting its length, and fixing spelling and grammar.

Create whole workspaces, locate relevant content and more with Joule

At the end of last year, Joule became integrated into SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition, bringing the AI assistant’s conversational intelligence right to your workspace. Now, Joule supports three new, frequently used scenarios:

  • Transactional: Create workspaces based on templates, upload content, or invite users to become members.
  • Navigational: Search for specific workspaces that you manage or are related to keywords.
  • Informational: Search for a specific user and show their profile.

You can trigger each of these scenarios through the familiar conversational experience with Joule, executing more tasks and finding key information faster.

👉 See example prompts and learn more on the Help Portal.

👉 See all the latest releases from SAP Build Work Zone.

Updates in Mobile: Seamless SSO, end-user support, MDK 26.6 & more

SAP Mobile Start’s rebranding, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on

SAP Mobile Start has been the established native mobile entry point for your company’s systems and daily tasks, including content from SAP Build Work Zone. The app has now been rebranded as the Joule Work mobile app. While the core functionalities remain the same as before, the new app includes an updated Joule icon, full-screen interface, and voice dictation of prompts. 

With version 3.0, Joule Work mobile app also introduces a streamlined single sign-on for opening web applications in the in-app browser. For organizations with large mobile workforces, this eliminates the need for repetitive credential entry and creates a seamless workflow across applications. Learn more about these updates in the blog posts by @ThiloBerndt and @thomas-bruckner.

  • SAP Mobile Start - Rebranding of the app, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on functionality
  • Joule Work mobile app version 3.1 is out
  • Seamless Mobile-to-Web Single Sign-On with the Joule Work mobile app

Customizing in-app support for end users in Joule Work

Getting technical support for mobile app issues is a common frustration that often causes users to turn to the wrong channels, such as app store reviews or generic feedback forms. It’s now possible for companies to add support information, such as contact details, ticketing systems and resources, directly to your Joule Work mobile app and configure it in SAP Mobile Services. This enhancement transforms how your end users can access help when they need it most.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @ivan_chaushev.

AI feedback forms, extended compliance checks and more in MDK Client 26.6

The latest release of Mobile development kit (MDK) is now available, bringing several significant upgrades to security, compliance and embedded AI in native mobile app development. Device compliance checks have now been extended to before a user onboards, allowing for earlier detection of compromised devices and stronger security. Enhancements to AI are also ongoing, including a new control that enables quick user feedback on AI-generated content.

👉 See the complete list of new MDK features in this blog post by @Jitendra_Kansal.

New CAP plugins for AI recommendations & improved traceability

RPT-1 generated recommendations with CAP AI plugin for node.js 

In pro-code development, SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) continues to provide productivity gains by allowing developers to reduce boilerplate code and focus on problem solving. The new SAP CAP AI plugin for node.js provides recommendations powered by RPT-1 within the in-app UI. The plugin supports multi-tenancy, simplifies AI Core configuration, and inherently embeds the enterprise readiness of CAP out-of-the-box.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @rupamaity.

New and improved change tracking plugin

Version 2.0 of the change tracking plugin for CAP has been released and brings major improvements to how enterprise applications capture and visualize data changes. The new version includes tree table visualization, support for CDS Expression Language (CXL), and a fundamentally redesigned architecture. These changes bring significant upgrades to performance and traceability, allowing developers to exercise more control over changes and customizations.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @rupamaity.

Streamlined SAPUI5 development with AI and developer extensibility

UI5 plugins for modernization and coding agents in GitHub Copilot

Keeping your SAPUI5 app up to date just got easier. The new UI5 Modernization Plugin automates the most time-consuming parts of app modernization: replacing deprecated APIs, eliminating global namespaces, and much more. The plugin is available for Claude Code as well as GitHub Copilot. Also now available for GitHub Copilot are UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents (previously known as UI5 Plugins for Claude). These bring up-to-date SAPUI5 knowledge directly into your AI-assisted development workflow.

The latest version of the UI5 MCP Server is also now available and supports the development of UI Integration Cards. It includes three new tools to assist Large Language Models (LLMs) in creating new cards from scratch or editing existing ones, following the best practices for card development. Learn more about these plugins:

  • Introducing the UI5 Modernization Plugin: Modernize Your OpenUI5/SAPUI5 App in Hours, Not Weeks
  • UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents: Now Supporting GitHub Copilot
  • UI5 MCP Server Got Extended for UI Integration Cards Generation

Key User Adaptation & Transitions for UI5 TypeScript developers

With key user adaptation, business users can change the UI of SAP Fiori standard apps directly in the runtime app. If more complex changes are needed, there now is a seamless transition from key user to developer extensibility in SAP Build.

TypeScript is going through its biggest transition in years. If you build SAPUI5 applications with TypeScript, we have provided a guide for developers around these changes.

👉 Learn more about key user adaptation and the TypeScript transition.

Extended visibility and proactive monitoring in process automation

Avoid manual error-tracing with automated exception handling.

Enhanced process visibility with instance-level attributes

Operational teams often lack business context in process monitoring. For a business user, this can make it difficult to see key details like approaching deadlines or decisions taken by the previous approver. Now with instance-level attributes enabled, you can add meaningful business context to process instances, allowing users to easily search, filter, group, and analyze processes. This allows teams to improve their decision-making and reduce the risk of breaching SLAs.

👉 Learn more about instance-level attributes in the Help Portal.

Process group connections & other updates

More updates are now available in process automation, focused on improved visibility, proactive monitoring, and enhanced governance. Here are some highlights:

  • Process group connections (1-to-many): Organizations often need visibility across related processes, not just individual workflows. This enables end-to-end monitoring by linking a primary process with its associated downstream processes, providing a more complete view of business execution.
  • Notifications in Visibility Scenarios: Proactive email alerts notify users about processing failures, skipped instances, or event thresholds before they impact operations. This reduces manual monitoring and helps teams respond to issues faster.
  • Environment Filters: New controls allow users to view or restrict process visibility by environment. This improves governance and makes it easier to manage and analyze process data across development, test, and production landscapes.
  • Event handler to catch Action errors: Introduces configurable Retry mechanisms and structured exception handling at the Action level. This allows organizations to systematically manage errors, while reducing manual and time-consuming troubleshooting efforts.

👉 See all the latest releases and documentation on the Help Portal.

Looking ahead to the new Joule Studio

If you're looking to learn more about the new Joule Studio and how you can use it to start creating AI agents, we have great news: the learning journey Introducing Joule Studio is now available! With this free course, you'll be able to understand the bigger picture and how Joule Studio fits into SAP Business AI Platform. You'll also get to know what you can do with Joule Studio and Joule Work, how you can use it to create agents and automate workflows with n8n.

👉 Start learning today: Introducing Joule Studio

entry.title: Governing the End-to-End Service Cycle in SAP S/4HANA Service with SAP Joule Agents
entry.content:
Introduction

Service is one of the most demanding business processes in SAP S/4HANA.

Every customer interaction can turn into a Service Request, a Service Quotation, a Service Order, and eventually a Service Confirmation — each with its own mandatory data, its own upstream dependency, and its own point at which mistakes become expensive.

Even with modern Fiori experiences, service teams still spend a large part of their day navigating between apps, resolving Business Partners, checking statuses, and validating whether upstream documents are actually ready. When something is missed, the cost is not just rework — it is billing errors, compliance gaps, and eroded customer trust.

With the arrival of SAP Joule, SAP Joule Studio, and the Generative AI Hub in SAP AI Core, we now have a governed way to bring intelligent, conversational experiences directly into these processes — without stepping outside SAP's business rules.

This blog walks through three tightly integrated SAP Joule Agents that together govern the complete service cycle in SAP S/4HANA — a Service Intake Assistant, a Service Order Assistant, and a Service Confirmation Assistant — and shares the design principles and lessons learned from building them.

If you are new to SAP Joule Studio or the Generative AI Hub, the SAP resources linked at the end of this blog are a good place to start before diving in.

The Business Challenge:

Service organizations across Industrial Equipment, Engineering Services, Field Service, and Utilities consistently report the same pain points across the service cycle:

  • Manual navigation and fragmented validation during intake
  • Ambiguous Business Partner resolution leading to downstream billing issues
  • Service Orders created without valid upstream commercial references
  • Premature order releases and uncontrolled updates
  • Planned values reused as actuals during confirmation
  • Irreversible service closure executed without readiness checks
  • Missing audit trail across intake, order, and confirmation stages

The net result: slower cycle times, higher operational risk, weaker first-time-right rates, and financial data that Finance and Analytics cannot fully trust.

What service teams need is not another chatbot — it is a governed, conversational execution layer that respects SAP's business rules at every step of the cycle.

The Three Service Management Agents:

Three SAP Joule Agents, one governed lifecycle.

Figure 1 — End-to-end Service Management cycle governed by the three SAP Joule Agents: Service Intake Assistant, Service Order Assistant, and Service Confirmation Assistant. Every stage follows the same pattern — natural language in, governed validation, backend-confirmed outcome.

1. Service Intake Assistant:

The starting point of the cycle. This agent delivers a governed, AI-assisted intake experience for Service Requests and Service Quotations. It resolves Business Partners from SAP master data, validates mandatory intake information in a single consolidated step, and creates or updates service documents only after successful SAP backend execution — ensuring first-time-right intake and eliminating incomplete service documents at the source.

Because the Service Intake Assistant is the entry point to the entire service cycle, the following walkthrough illustrates its behavior in depth. The same conversational, consolidated-validation, backend-confirmed pattern is then applied consistently by the Service Order Assistant and the Service Confirmation Assistant across the rest of the lifecycle.

 

 

Screenshot 1 — Launching the Service Intake Assistant inside SAP Joule for a natural-language conversation with a service user.

The interaction begins with a simple natural-language request from the service user. The agent then resolves the Business Partner directly against SAP master data and retrieves the partner's existing service documents before creating anything new.

Screenshot 2 — The Service Intake Assistant resolves the Business Partner against SAP master data and retrieves all existing Service Requests for the partner before any new document is created.

Once the Business Partner is confirmed, the agent gathers every mandatory field in a single consolidated interaction rather than a long back-and-forth. If anything is missing, ambiguous, or blocking, the agent communicates it transparently along with what has already been collected — nothing is assumed, nothing is fabricated.

 

Screenshot 3 — Consolidated single-step validation in action: the agent displays every field already available and transparently flags the single missing mandatory input before proceeding to backend execution.

Only when all validations pass does the agent invoke the corresponding SAP backend operation. Success is confirmed only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid document number — never before.

 

 

Screenshot 4 — Backend-confirmed Service Request creation in SAP S/4HANA Service Management — the agent confirms success only after the SAP backend returns a valid document number.

The same governed pattern applies to Service Quotations, which the agent creates against the confirmed Business Partner with full pricing, quantity, validity, and status returned directly from SAP.

 

Screenshot 5 — Backend-confirmed Service Quotation creation for the same Business Partner, with pricing, quantity, validity, and status returned directly from SAP S/4HANA.

Updates to existing service documents follow the same discipline. The agent applies the change only after backend confirmation and presents a clear before-and-after view so that the update is auditable.

 

Screenshot 6 — Controlled update of an existing Service Quotation, with the agent showing the previous and new values only after backend confirmation of the update. 

2. Service Order Assistant:

The execution governance layer of the cycle. This agent controls Service Order creation, updates, and release. It ensures that Service Orders are created only from valid upstream Service Quotation references, enforces consolidated dependency validation, and executes release only when SAP backend conditions are met — eliminating unauthorized or orphan Service Orders and preventing premature releases.

The following walkthrough illustrates the Service Order lifecycle end-to-end: retrieve, create, update, and release.

 

Screenshot 7 — Launching the Service Order Assistant inside SAP Joule, which supports the complete Service Order lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and release.

Before any Service Order is created, the agent enforces its most important governance rule — the presence of a valid upstream Service Quotation reference. Only after the Quotation is confirmed does the agent request the mandatory fields for Service Order creation.

 

Screenshot 8 — Upstream dependency enforcement: the Service Order Assistant validates the referenced Service Quotation before collecting the mandatory fields required for Service Order creation.

When all validations pass, the agent executes the create operation and confirms success only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Order number, linked back to the originating Quotation for traceability.

 

Screenshot 9 — Backend-confirmed Service Order creation, linked to the upstream Service Quotation. Success is confirmed only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Order number.

Updates to existing Service Orders follow the same discipline. The agent applies changes only after backend confirmation and presents a clear before-and-after view for auditability.

 

Screenshot 10 — Controlled item-level update of the Service Order, with the agent showing previous and new values only after successful backend confirmation.

Release is treated as a distinct governance event. Before any release is executed, the agent runs a full Release Readiness Assessment — verifying every prerequisite and reporting blockers transparently. Release is permitted only when the assessment returns clean.

 

Screenshot 11 — Release Readiness Assessment: the Service Order Assistant verifies every release prerequisite (existence, status, upstream reference, organizational data, service details) and reports "no blockers detected" before any release is executed.

Once released, the Service Order is officially available for work scheduling and execution — which is the natural handoff cue to the Service Confirmation Assistant, the third agent in the cycle.

 

Screenshot 12 — Backend-confirmed release of the Service Order in SAP S/4HANA. The agent confirms release status, pricing document creation, and execution readiness only after successful backend validation — completing the governed handoff into service execution.

3. Service Confirmation Assistant:

The closure layer of the cycle. This agent governs actuals-based Service Confirmation creation, updates, and final completion. It ensures that confirmations are recorded only against valid, released Service Orders, enforces a clear separation between planned and actual execution data, and performs final completion only after SAP backend confirmation — treating closure as the irreversible business action it is.

The following walkthrough illustrates the complete Service Confirmation lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and complete.

 

Screenshot 13 — Launching the Service Confirmation Assistant inside SAP Joule. The agent supports the complete Service Confirmation lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and — as an explicitly irreversible action — complete.

Before any confirmation is created, the agent verifies the upstream Service Order's release status and reports whether confirmations already exist. This is the discipline that keeps actuals aligned to real, released work.

 

Screenshot 14 — Before any confirmation is created, the agent retrieves the upstream Service Order's release status and reports the existing confirmation state — enforcing the rule that actuals can only be recorded against valid, released Service Orders.

Once readiness is confirmed, the agent gathers every mandatory field in a single consolidated request — the same design pattern applied by the Intake and Order Assistants.

 

Screenshot 15 — Consolidated mandatory field collection for Service Confirmation creation — the same single-step validation pattern applied by every agent in the Service Management set.

The agent then executes the create operation and confirms success only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Confirmation ID, linked back to the released Service Order for full traceability.

 

Screenshot 16 — Backend-confirmed Service Confirmation creation in SAP S/4HANA, linked back to the released Service Order. Success is confirmed only after the SAP backend returns a valid Service Confirmation ID.

Updates continue to follow the same auditable discipline as the previous agents in the cycle — with a clean before-and-after view after every backend-confirmed change.

 

Screenshot 17 — Controlled update of an existing Service Confirmation, with the agent presenting previous and new values only after backend confirmation — the same auditability pattern applied across the cycle.

Finally, completion is treated as the irreversible business action that it is. The agent captures actual service duration and start time, performs final completion only after SAP backend confirmation, and explicitly communicates that the confirmation is finalized and cannot be undone.

Screenshot 18 — Irreversible Service Confirmation completion in SAP S/4HANA. The agent records actual service duration and start time, finalizes the confirmation as an audit-ready, non-reversible business action, and closes the end-to-end service cycle that began with the Service Intake Assistant.

How the Agents Work Together:

The Service Intake walkthrough above illustrated a design pattern that all three agents share: natural language in, governed SAP execution out.

The Service Order Assistant applies the same discipline to Service Order creation, updates, and release — refusing to create orders without a valid upstream Service Quotation reference, enforcing consolidated dependency validation, and releasing only when SAP backend conditions are met.

The Service Confirmation Assistant closes the loop with the same rigor — recording actual only against released Service Orders, separating planned and actual data cleanly, and performing final closure only after SAP backend confirmation.

The result is a service lifecycle in which no stage can be skipped, and no stage can be executed without the previous one being genuinely valid — exactly what enterprise-grade service governance requires.

 

Figure 2 — Shared design pattern across all three agents: user intent expressed in natural language, consolidated context and validation, governed SAP backend execution, and confirmed outcome that hands off cleanly to the next stage of the service lifecycle.

Solution Architecture:

All three agents are built entirely on SAP's AI and application stack.

Figure 3 — Technical realization on SAP Business Technology Platform: SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Build Process Automation, SAP Joule Studio, SAP AI Core, and SAP Cloud Identity Services deployed in a multi-cloud subaccount, connected to on-premises SAP S/4HANA or SAP ECC through Cloud Connector via OData/REST and HTTPS.

Architecture Components:

  • SAP Joule — Conversational interaction layer for service users.
  • SAP Joule Studio — Design, configure, and orchestrate each agent, its skills, guardrails, and prompts.
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) — Runtime, identity, and extensibility foundation shared across the agent set.
  • Generative AI Hub in SAP AI Core — Governed access to large language models, prompt orchestration, and AI governance.
  • SAP S/4HANA Service Management — System of record for Business Partners, Service Requests, Service Quotations, Service Orders, and Service Confirmations.

Business Value:

Adopting the three Service Management agents together delivers value that no single-point automation can match:

  • First-time-right service intake — Governed data collection and Business Partner resolution eliminate incomplete Service Requests and Quotations at the source.
  • Controlled, traceable execution — Every Service Order is created and released from a valid upstream reference, preventing unauthorized or orphan orders.
  • Accurate, irreversible closure — Confirmations capture real actuals against released orders, protecting billing accuracy and audit readiness.
  • Faster cycle times — Removing manual navigation and fragmented validation shortens intake, execution, and closure across the lifecycle.
  • Trustworthy service data — Finance and Analytics receive service data that reflects real backend execution, not optimistic UI states.
  • Reduced operational risk — Governance is enforced by design across every stage, not left to individual user discipline.

Example Use Cases:

  • A Service Desk Operator captures a customer request and creates a Service Request through a natural-language conversation, without navigating any Fiori app.
  • A Service Order Coordinator converts an approved Service Quotation into a controlled Service Order and releases it once all dependencies are satisfied.
  • A Field Service Technician records actual time and effort against a released Service Order and closes the service as a final, irreversible action.
  • A Customer Service Manager retrieves and reviews open Service Quotations, Service Orders, or Service Confirmations for a specific Business Partner through a single conversational query.
  • An Operations Lead uses the same conversational surface across all three agents to move a service case cleanly from request to closure.

Lessons Learned Building an End-to-End Agent Set:

Building not one but three interlinked SAP Joule Agents surfaced lessons that are directly relevant to anyone building on SAP Joule Studio and the Generative AI Hub.

  1. Design for the Cycle, Not for the Agent

The single biggest architectural decision was to design the three agents as a coherent set rather than three isolated agents. Handoffs between intake, order, and confirmation are where enterprise value is created — and where governance is most easily lost.

  1. Governance Is Non-Negotiable at Every Stage

Every stage of the service cycle has its own point at which mistakes become expensive. The agents therefore enforce validation, upstream references, and backend confirmation at every stage, not just at the point of creation.

  1. Consolidated Validation Beats Iterative Prompting

Users abandon agents that ask for information one field at a time. Collecting and validating in a single consolidated step turned out to be one of the highest-impact UX decisions across all three agents.

  1. Never Confirm Before the Backend Confirms

Optimistic confirmations erode trust immediately. Every agent in the set returns success only when SAP S/4HANA returns a valid outcome — even when that makes the conversation slightly longer.

  1. Actuals Deserve the Same Rigor as Creation

Service Confirmations are the stage where planned and actual data are most often confused. Treating actuals capture and final closure with the same rigor as intake and order creation was essential to protect billing accuracy and audit readiness.

  1. A Shared Design Language Accelerates Everything

Because all three agents follow the same natural-language-in, backend-confirmed-out pattern, users learn one interaction model and reuse it across the entire cycle — which is a significant adoption advantage.

Conclusion:

The Service Intake Assistant, Service Order Assistant, and Service Confirmation Assistant together demonstrate how SAP Business AI, SAP Joule, SAP Joule Studio, and the SAP Generative AI Hub can be used to govern an entire enterprise business cycle — not just automate a single step.

The three agents show that agentic AI on SAP does not have to trade off governance for productivity. When designed as a coherent set, SAP Joule Agents can deliver faster execution, higher data quality, controlled handoffs, and audit-ready outcomes across the full service cycle.

As enterprises accelerate their SAP Business AI journeys, an end-to-end agent set like this represents a practical, production-ready path to governed, conversational service execution.

If you are exploring SAP Joule Studio or building your first SAP Joule Agent, I would be happy to hear how you are approaching governance, consolidated validation, and backend confirmation in your own designs.

Please share your thoughts in the comments below.

About the Author:

The author is a Lead Architect working on SAP Business AI, SAP Joule, and SAP BTP, focused on the design and delivery of governed SAP Joule Agents for enterprise business processes on SAP S/4HANA.

entry.title: How to Transform Business Rules into Conversational Experiences Using SAP BPA and SAP Joule Studio
entry.content:
Introduction

"Am I eligible?"

"Can I proceed with this request?"

"Do I need approvals?"

"What should I do next?"

Organizations already have the answers tothese questions. They exist in policies, SOPs, decision matrices, knowledge repositories, and business processes.

The challenge is not defining business rules it is making them accessible, consistent, and available when users need guidance.

With SAP Joule Studio, organizations can deliver a conversational experience through natural language interactions. However, this raises an important architectural question:

Should AI make business decisions, or should AI provide access to decisions that are already governed by enterprise business rules?

This approach keeps decision logic governed and auditable while allowing users to interact through natural language.

By combining SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Joule Studio, business decisions remain governed and auditable, while users receive conversational guidance based on structured and approved business rules.

This architecture pattern is reusable across any rule-driven scenario, including eligibility assessments, approvals, compliance checks, policy guidance, benefits administration, procurement workflows, and employee self-service processes.

Business Rules Decide. SAP Joule Makes Them Conversational.

Why Keep Business Rules Outside Prompts?

When building AI assistants, it can be tempting to embed business logic directly into prompts. While this may work for simple use cases, it quickly becomes difficult to govern, audit, and maintain as complexity grows.

 Component Responsibilities

Component

Responsibility

SAP Joule Studio

Conversational experience

SAP Build Process Automation

Business decisioning and rule execution

Joule Responses

User-friendly guidance

 This blog demonstrates a practical architecture pattern for transforming governed business rules into conversational experiences using SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Joule Studio.

Business Scenario

Consider an employee asking: "Am I eligible for an international assignment?" SAP Build Process Automation evaluates the eligibility rules and returns a structured decision. SAP Joule Studio then converts the outcome into clear, conversational guidance.

Business Impact

  • Consistent decisioning
  • Better governance
  • Faster user support
  • Reusable services
  • Simpler AI interactions

Building the Governed Decision Layer with SAP Build Process Automation

Step 1: Create SAP Build Process Automation Project

Navigate to-->SAP Build Lobby-->Create-->Process Automation-->Create a new BPA project-->Project Name-->Save

This project will contain all rule-related artifacts and act as the business decisioning layer.

Step 2: Create Decision Services

Navigate to BPA Project--> Create-->Select Decision-->Decision Name-->Description-->Create Input Parameter--> create output parameter (if single output is required)

Step 3: Create a Reusable Result Data Type

Create a custom data type to bundle multiple decision outputs into a structured response.

Select a Datatype-->Define Name-->Add parameters with required datatype-->Save-->Close

Step 4: Rule Creation

Click Rule-->Enter Rule Name-->Description-->Select Hit policy (First match) -->Category(default)-->Next Step-->Select Input -->Next-->select output-->Select output-->Review-->Finish

Step 5: Create Rule Records

Open the Rule -->Add records manually or use Import/Export for bulk maintenance-->Save

Step 6: Create BPA Process

Create a BPA Process to orchestrate one or more Decisions and expose them as a reusable service.

Goto Create-->Process-->Add Decision(s)--> Define input/Output Variable-->click each decision box-->Map input and output variable for each decision block-->Save-->Release-->Deploy

Step 7: Publish the Process

Navigate to SAP Build Lobby -->Select Project -->Latest Version -->More Actions (⋮) --> Publish to Library.

Step 8: Test and Validate the Process

Navigate to Control Tower--> Select Environment -->Open Trigger-->Add Trigger (convert the process into API trigger) -->Save -->Open Process and Workflow--> Select process-->select Start a New Instance -->Paste JSON Payload → Run-->open monitoring-->Review the Execution Status and Outputs

Building the Conversational Layer with SAP Joule Studio

Step 1: Create Joule Project

Navigate to SAP Build Lobby -->Create -->Joule Agent and Skills -->Enter Project Name and Description -->Save.

Step 2: Add BPA Dependencies

Open Joule Project -->Click Manage Project Properties --> Dependencies --> Add -->Select the Published BPA Process/Action -->Save

Why? To allow Joule to consume business decision services without duplicating business logic.

Step 3: Invoke the BPA Process

Open the Joule Skill -->Add (+) -->Run Process -->Select the Published BPA Process--> Define Input and Output Variables -->Map the Required Process Inputs -->Save.

Important: Joule should call the process, not individual decision

Step 4: Store Process Outputs

Navigate to Joule Skill -->Add (+) -->Set Variable --> Create Custom Variables -->Select Dynamic Value -->Map Process Output Fields -->Save

Note: When using bundled custom data types, direct dynamic binding may not always be available. Using Set Variable simplifies access to individual output fields.

Step 5: Add Conditional Responses

Open the Joule Skill --> Add (+) -->Condition Branch --> Configure Conditions using the Condition Editor --> Add Response Messages for Each Outcome -->Save -->Release--> Deploy.

Step 6: Test the Conversational Experience

Open the Joule Test Panel --> Enter a sample user query --> Execute the Skill -->Verify process invocation, response generation, and expected business outcomes.

Key Challenges

Pitfall

How to address

Users enter information differently

Normalize inputs before evaluation

Rules become difficult to manage

Split logic into modular decisions

Some requests don't match any rule

Define default outcomes

AI may return inconsistent answers

Use BPA as the source of truth

Technical outputs are hard to understand

Convert outcomes into business guidance

Rules change frequently

Keep decisioning separate from conversation

 Conclusion

The goal is not to let AI make business decisions, but to make governed decisions easier to access. By combining SAP Build Process Automation with SAP Joule Studio, organizations can transform complex rules into intuitive conversational experiences.

SAP Build Process Automation decides. Joule makes it conversational.
